role_hints.role: Engineers and technical delivery staff (developers, systems engineers)
role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 23.9% of wage bill + 15-1299 Computer Occupations All Other 3.1% + 15-1253 Software QA 2.2% (role_mix is 4-digit 5415; for this code — systems design/integration/MSP — some of the 'developer' share is really systems/network engineers)
role_hints.note: Scripting, integration glue code, configuration, patching automation, runbook drafting and documentation are heavily AI-exposed; solution architecture judgment, client-specific environment knowledge and on-site/hardware work persist. For MSP-model firms, read this role as centralized services engineers — the tier where AI automation (self-healing endpoints, automated remediation) is already commercial.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1232 Computer User Support Specialists 3.3% at 4-digit — materially understated for MSP-heavy 541512, where support desks are the largest headcount block; est. ~8-12% at 6-digit
role_hints.note: Tier-1 ticket resolution, password resets, onboarding/offboarding — the most directly AI-automatable cost line in an MSP, and the one AI service-desk products already target. Physical desk-side/field visits are the non-automatable residue.
Assessments, migration planning docs and compliance gap analyses automate substantially; stakeholder work and vendor selection judgment persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-3021 Computer & Information Systems Managers 8.3% + 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 3.0%
role_hints.note: vCIO/technology-alignment reviews, project coordination and reporting automate in part; the trusted-advisor QBR relationship is the retention mechanism and persists.
Owner-operators hold the client relationships in sub-$10M firms; internal ops automate.
Proposal/quote production and renewals paperwork automate; local relationship selling to SMB owners persists.

role_hints.role: Admin, dispatch, finance and unallocated tail
role_hints.oews_basis: long tail beyond the listed top-10 (top-10 sums to ~61% of wages), est. remainder ~28-32%
role_hints.note: Ticketing admin, dispatch, procurement/quoting, invoicing — heavily automatable; part of the tail is additional field/bench technicians (physical) — allocate between patterns.

source_hints.source: Kaseya Global MSP Benchmark Report (annual, ~1,000 MSP respondents)
source_hints.why: Primary survey data on MSP tooling, automation and AI adoption, revenue mix and profitability — direct input for current_adoption_pct.

source_hints.source: ConnectWise/IT Nation MSP research and Service Leadership publications
source_hints.why: The other major MSP-platform vendor's research arm; corroborating adoption and operational-maturity data.

source_hints.source: Service Leadership Index (ConnectWise) — MSP financial benchmark
source_hints.why: The longest-running benchmark of MSP EBITDA%, pricing models (per-seat/per-device MRR vs. T&M) and best-in-class vs. median economics — the capture question's evidence base.

source_hints.source: Solganick & Co. MSP/IT services M&A reports
source_hints.why: Tracked 400+ MSP/MSSP transactions in 2025 with disclosed-value aggregates — deal counts for B and multiples for M.

source_hints.source: Aventis Advisors MSP valuation multiples reports
source_hints.why: Published small-vs-platform MSP EBITDA multiple ranges — the buy/exit multiple arbitrage evidence.

source_hints.source: Acquisition announcements of the major platforms: Evergreen Services Group (Lyra/Pine/Cedar), New Charter Technologies, Ntiva, Thrive, Integris
source_hints.why: The named active consolidators — Evergreen alone confirmed ~47 acquisitions in 2025; primary evidence for active_consolidators.

source_hints.source: MSP owner-demographic data via Service Leadership / IT Nation conference research and broker commentary
source_hints.why: Many small MSPs are founder-owned dating to the 1990s-2000s break-fix era; verify owner-age claims here rather than assuming sector-54 norms.

capture_questions: The MSP model bills per-seat or per-endpoint monthly recurring fees under 1-3 year agreements — when AI service desks and self-healing endpoint automation cut delivery cost per seat, the operator keeps the spread as margin until competitive repricing; but the same billing unit creates a second channel: if AI shrinks client headcount, billed seats shrink with it. What is the evidence on both — are per-seat rates holding or compressing in competitive renewals, and are seat counts at SMB clients starting to decline?

Who owns the client relationship — the firm (contract, documentation, QBR cadence, tooling lock-in) or the owner/lead engineer personally? MSP agreements, environment documentation and security tooling create real switching costs; assess how much survives an acquisition and whether platform roll-ups' retention data confirms it.

The RMM/PSA/security vendors (Kaseya, ConnectWise, Microsoft) sit upstream of every MSP — as they embed autonomous remediation and AI service desks into the platforms themselves, do they capture the automation value via license pricing (squeezing MSP gross margin), or even sell AI-run IT management directly to SMBs, disintermediating the MSP layer?

What realistically stops an SMB from bypassing its MSP with generic AI plus SaaS defaults — is the durable moat security/compliance accountability (cyber insurance requirements, CMMC/HIPAA attestations, incident response liability) rather than routine IT labor, and what share of MSP revenue is already security/compliance-anchored?

Hardware resale and project work (migrations, installs) still make up a large minority of small-MSP revenue — those are competitively bid and pass AI savings through; what is the MRR vs. project/resale split in the target tier, since capture economics differ completely between them?
terminal_value_question: SMBs will still need their IT to run, but the 5-10 year question is who operates the AI that runs it: if the MSP becomes the accountable operator of autonomous IT/security agents for clients too small to do it themselves, AI cuts the operator's costs and per-seat MRR holds as an insurance-like fee; if instead the RMM/security platform vendors ship autonomous management that SMBs consume directly (or AI collapses SMB software estates into a few self-managing SaaS suites), the MSP's product is replaced and the seat base it bills evaporates. The deciding mechanisms are concrete — cyber-insurance and compliance attestation requirements naming an accountable provider, and platform vendors' channel economics (do Kaseya/ConnectWise/Microsoft need the MSP channel or compete with it?). Which way does current evidence point, and does per-seat billing survive contact with AI-shrunk client headcounts?
special_notes: This code hosts the flagship MSP roll-up wave: ~169+ disclosed MSP deals in 2025 with PE in ~69%, platforms (Evergreen, New Charter, Ntiva, Thrive, Integris) buying at 4-7x small-firm EBITDA and trading at 10-13x — prime evidence for B and M, and per rule 2 absolutely inadmissible as evidence for V or A. Keep the wave out of automatability and adoption scoring.

Sibling boundaries (the run WILL hit contaminated data): 541512 = designing and integrating computer systems — where most MSPs, systems integrators and VARs-with-services self-classify; 541511 = writing custom code to spec (dev shops); 541513 = ongoing management of a client's OWN facilities/data-center operations (outsourced IT ops); 541519 = residual services (software installation, disaster recovery). MSP benchmarks (Kaseya, Service Leadership) and deal trackers sample by business model, not NAICS — expect their populations to span 541511/541513/541519 and treat NAICS-coded government data vs. industry surveys as different frames.

Revenue-mix normalization trap: small-MSP receipts blend high-margin MRR, low-margin hardware/license resale (pass-through inflates revenue, deflates labor_share — the record's 0.6444 reflects this) and lumpy project work. Multiples are quoted on adjusted EBITDA of MRR-weighted books; a resale-heavy firm at the same revenue is worth half as much. Never apply MRR-book multiples to blended-revenue firms.

EBITDA normalization: owner-operator comp inside SDE for sub-$2M-EBITDA MSPs; Service Leadership's own data shows best-in-class ~2.5x median EBITDA% — wide dispersion means 'average margin' hides the target-selection reality.

Per-seat MRR is a capture-POSITIVE pricing structure near-term (fees decoupled from hours), unusual in sector 54 — but model the seat-count channel explicitly in pi_dist: AI-driven client headcount reduction shrinks the billing base even if rates hold.
The 2022 receipts vintage predates both the AI service-desk products and the 2024-2025 deal-tempo peak; check current (2025-2026) deal pace and pricing rather than extrapolating the dataset era.
